meta.title: TB-DF-7: Core Authoring, Analysis, and Publishing

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. when would a pivot in the Data Source page be most appropriate?

options.text: When wide month columns need to become attribute-value rows
When two database tables need an inner join
When a workbook must be published
When a continuous field should become discrete

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because pivoting in Tableau's Data Source page reshapes wide data—such as one column per month—into a long format with a single attribute column (Month) and a value column (Sales), which is the structure Tableau's shelf-based analysis expects.

**B** is incorrect because joining tables uses the join editor in the Data Source page canvas, not the pivot feature; inner join is a join type that keeps only matching rows.

**C** is incorrect because publishing is a server action performed through the File or Server menu after the workbook is built; it does not involve pivoting.

**D** is incorrect because changing a field from continuous to discrete is done by right-clicking the field pill in a view or in the Data pane; it is not performed through the Data Source pivot feature.

See [Tableau Help: Pivot data from columns to rows](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/pivot.htm).
questions.reference: [Tableau Help: Pivot data from columns to rows](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/pivot.htm)

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. which TWO statements about joins in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Inner joins keep only records with matches on both sides
Joins can be used only with text files
A join always stacks rows from one table under another
A join combines columns from tables based on matching fields

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because joins use a key relationship to horizontally combine fields from two or more tables, producing a wider result set where each row contains columns from all joined tables.

**B** is correct because an inner join filters the result to only include rows where the key value exists in both tables, discarding unmatched rows from either side.

**C** is incorrect because stacking rows is the behavior of a union, not a join; joins combine columns, not rows.

**D** is incorrect because Tableau supports joins across many source types including Excel, databases, and cloud warehouses; joins are not restricted to text files.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. A chart needs different colors for categories. Which Marks card property should the analyst use?
questions.explanation: Color is correct because the Color property controls mark hue and color encoding. Detail changes granularity, Tooltip affects hover text, and Rows affects layout.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is the purpose of an `IF` or `CASE` calculation in Tableau?

options.text: To return different results based on logical conditions
To create a union between tables

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `IF` and `CASE` are conditional logic functions; an `IF` expression evaluates Boolean conditions in sequence and returns the value associated with the first true branch, while `CASE` matches a field's value against a list of options, both enabling branching logic within a calculated field.

**B** is incorrect because unions are created in the Data Source page by dragging or selecting additional tables to append; they are not produced by IF or CASE calculations.

**C** is incorrect because publishing extracts is an action performed through the Server or File menu to save a .hyper file to Tableau Server or Cloud; it is not a calculation type.

**D** is incorrect because project permissions are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud by site administrators; they are not defined through workbook calculations.

See [Tableau Help: Create calculated fields](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/calculations_calculatedfields.htm).

questions.stem: In Tableau, how are dimensions typically used in a view?

options.text: They usually segment or label the data
They are always continuous green pills

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because dimensions are categorical fields that define the level of detail in a view; they create headers, partition marks, and labels that segment quantitative data by attributes such as product, region, or date.

**B** is incorrect because dimensions can be filtered using dimension filters (selecting or excluding specific member values); filtering is one of the most common uses of dimension fields.

**C** is incorrect because dimensions are typically displayed as blue discrete pills; continuous fields (usually measures or dates used as continuous) appear as green pills.

**D** is incorrect because workbook permissions are managed through Tableau Server or Cloud administrative settings; dimensions are data fields used for analysis, not for permission storage.

questions.stem: How does a set differ from a group in Tableau?

options.text: A set defines membership that can be dynamic or conditional, while a group combines members into named categories
A set is only for maps, while a group is only for tables
A set is always numeric, while a group is always date-based

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a set partitions members of a dimension into an In group and an Out group based on fixed selection, a condition, or a top-N rule that can update dynamically as the data changes, while a group manually combines selected members into a new named category that behaves as an additional dimension value.

**B** is incorrect because both sets and groups can be created from any dimension regardless of the visualization type; neither is restricted to maps or tables.

**C** is incorrect because sets and groups are created from any dimension type, including string, date, or numeric; neither is restricted to a specific data type.

**D** is incorrect because sets and groups serve meaningfully different purposes: sets drive In/Out logic usable in calculations and actions, while groups create new fixed category labels.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is a common use case for a highlight table?

options.text: Showing magnitude patterns across rows and columns with color encoding

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a highlight table is a text table where cells are colored by a measure's value, making it easy to scan rows and columns simultaneously and spot high and low values, outliers, or patterns across two categorical breakdowns.

**B** is incorrect because cross-database joins are configured in the Data Source page canvas; they are a data preparation task unrelated to chart type selection.

**C** is incorrect because subscriptions are a server-side delivery feature; they schedule email snapshots of views and are not related to highlight tables.

**D** is incorrect because cleaning spreadsheet headers is an import-time task handled by Data Interpreter or manual field renaming in the Data Source page; it is not a function of any chart type.

See [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm).
questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: A team needs to share a dashboard with offline users who do not have server access. Which option is most appropriate?

options.text: Distribute a packaged workbook for Tableau Reader when the use case fits
Use only a live connection to a private database
Require every user to edit calculated fields manually
Convert the dashboard into a context filter

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Tableau Reader is a free application that can open packaged workbooks (.twbx files) without requiring a Tableau Server or Cloud license; distributing the .twbx file allows offline users to view and interact with the dashboard using the bundled data.

**B** is incorrect because requiring end users to edit calculated fields assumes they have Tableau Desktop licenses and analytical expertise; it does not address the requirement of offline access for non-technical stakeholders.

**C** is incorrect because a context filter is a query-scoping feature within a worksheet; it cannot be converted into a shareable dashboard format.

**D** is incorrect because a live connection to a private database requires network access to the database server; offline users by definition cannot reach private network resources.

See [Tableau Help](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/save_savework_packagedworkbooks.htm) for the documented behavior.

questions.stem: What is a calculated field in Tableau?

options.text: A user-defined expression that creates a new field from existing data
A permission rule on a project
A type of workbook export only

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a calculated field is a new virtual column defined by a formula (using functions, operators, and existing fields) that Tableau evaluates at query time; it behaves like any other field and can be placed on shelves, used in filters, and referenced in other calculations.

**B** is incorrect because permission rules are administrative settings in Tableau Server or Cloud that control user access; they are not formula-based fields.

**C** is incorrect because workbook exports (PDF, image, CSV) are output formats; a calculated field is a persistent data definition, not a one-time export.

**D** is incorrect because static image annotations are fixed text or visual elements added to a view for context; they are not computed from data.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. a team is evaluating the next step. what is the main purpose of Data Interpreter in Tableau?

options.text: It cleans common Excel formatting issues such as title rows and footers during import
It creates level of detail expressions
It publishes extracts to Tableau Cloud automatically

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Data Interpreter analyzes spreadsheets at import and removes common formatting artifacts—such as merged header cells, title rows, and footer text—that would otherwise appear as data rows, producing a cleaner field structure for analysis.

**A** is incorrect because subscription scheduling is a server-side feature in Tableau Cloud and Tableau Server that sends email snapshots to users; it is not a Data Interpreter capability.

**C** is incorrect because publishing extracts to Tableau Cloud is a separate step performed through the Publish menu after connecting to and preparing data; Data Interpreter is an import-time cleaning tool.

**D** is incorrect because level of detail (LOD) expressions are custom calculations written by the author; they are not generated by Data Interpreter.

See [Connect to Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/basicconnectoverview.htm).

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which TWO statements about measures in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Measures are always discrete blue pills
Measures commonly represent quantitative values such as sales or profit
Measures are often aggregated in views by default
Measures can never be used in filters

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because when a measure is placed on a shelf, Tableau automatically applies an aggregation (SUM by default for numeric fields) to summarize the values at the level of detail defined by the dimensions in the view.

**B** is correct because measures are numeric fields that represent quantities analysts want to aggregate, compare, or calculate—such as Sales, Profit, Quantity, or Order Count.

**C** is incorrect because measures can be filtered using measure filters, which allow you to filter by an aggregate value (e.g., show only rows where SUM(Sales) > 1000) or at the row level before aggregation.

**D** is incorrect because measures placed on shelves as continuous fields appear as green pills; discrete blue pills indicate fields used as discrete (typically dimensions). A measure can be converted to discrete, but it is not always blue by default.

See [Tableau Help](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/datafields_typesandroles_datatypes.htm) for the documented behavior.

questions.stem: Which shelf on the Marks card controls whether text labels appear on marks?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because placing a field on the Label shelf of the Marks card adds that field's value as text displayed directly on or near each mark; the Label shelf also provides options to control label alignment, font, and visibility conditions.

**B** is incorrect because the Pages shelf creates a page-by-page animation or flip book of the view broken out by the values of the field placed there; it controls animated playback, not text labeling.

**C** is incorrect because the Filters shelf restricts which rows of data are included in the view; it does not control text annotation on marks.

**D** is incorrect because the Columns shelf determines what fields define the horizontal axis or column headers; it is a view structure shelf, not a mark-level labeling control.

See [Tableau Help](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/viewparts_marks_markproperties.htm) for the documented behavior.
questions.reference: [Control the Appearance of Marks in the View](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/viewparts_marks_markproperties.htm)

questions.stem: Why publish a data source separately from a workbook?

options.text: To prevent anyone from connecting to data
To let multiple workbooks reuse a governed source definition

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a published data source on Tableau Server or Cloud is a shared, versioned connection definition that multiple workbooks can reference; this means credentials, joins, and calculated fields are maintained in one place and all connected workbooks benefit from updates automatically.

**B** is incorrect because publishing a data source makes it more accessible to authorized users, not less; restricting access is handled through permissions, not through the publishing action itself.

**C** is incorrect because a published data source can use either a live connection or an extract; publishing does not disable extract functionality.

**D** is incorrect because field type assignments (measure vs. dimension) are set within the data source and are not changed by publishing; the assignment persists as defined.

See [Tableau Help](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/publish_workbooks_howto.htm) for the documented behavior.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. which chart type is most suitable for showing change in a measure over time?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a line chart encodes time on the horizontal axis and the measure on the vertical axis, with connected line segments that make trends, seasonality, and rate of change immediately visible to the viewer.

**B** is incorrect because a treemap uses nested rectangles sized by a measure to show part-to-whole relationships; it has no inherent time axis and does not communicate change over time effectively.

**C** is incorrect because packed bubbles encode magnitude as circle area without a temporal axis; without a time dimension, they cannot show how a measure changes over time.

**D** is incorrect because a highlight table uses color intensity in a matrix to show magnitude across row and column dimensions; while it can include a date dimension, it is optimized for cross-dimensional comparison, not for communicating trend direction.

See [Tableau Help](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm) for the documented behavior.
questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. Data must stay queried from the source system instead of being copied into an extract. What should the user choose?
questions.explanation: A live connection is correct because it queries the source system directly rather than using an extract copy. Story sheets, set actions, and packaged workbooks are unrelated to connection mode.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of Data Interpreter in Tableau?

options.text: It cleans common Excel formatting issues such as title rows and footers during import
It publishes extracts to Tableau Cloud automatically
It creates level of detail expressions

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Data Interpreter analyzes spreadsheets at import and removes common formatting artifacts—such as merged header cells, title rows, and footer text—that would otherwise appear as data rows, producing a cleaner field structure for analysis.

**A** is incorrect because subscription scheduling is a server-side feature in Tableau Cloud and Tableau Server that sends email snapshots to users; it is not a Data Interpreter capability.

**C** is incorrect because publishing extracts to Tableau Cloud is a separate step performed through the Publish menu after connecting to and preparing data; Data Interpreter is an import-time cleaning tool.

**D** is incorrect because level of detail (LOD) expressions are custom calculations written by the author; they are not generated by Data Interpreter.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. a design review asks for justification. why should an analyst be cautious with packed bubbles or treemaps for exact comparisons?

options.text: They are the only option for quantitative data
Area-based encodings are harder to compare precisely than aligned lengths

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because human perception judges area differences less accurately than length differences along a common baseline; viewers can reliably compare bar heights side by side but struggle to judge whether one circle or rectangle is 20% or 30% larger than another, making packed bubbles and treemaps poor choices when precise ranking or exact value comparison is required.

**B** is incorrect because packed bubbles and treemaps do display dimensions; the dimension field defines which group each bubble or rectangle represents.

**C** is incorrect because packed bubbles and treemaps are one option among many for quantitative data; bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, and other types are equally valid depending on the analytical goal.

**D** is incorrect because tooltips work on all Tableau mark types including circles and rectangles; packing bubbles or using a treemap does not disable tooltip display.

See [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm).
questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: What is the main benefit of a context filter before a computationally expensive top-N filter?

options.text: It can reduce the data considered by the later top-N logic
It converts live data into an extract automatically

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a context filter materializes a temporary sub-table; when the top-N filter evaluates next, it operates only over the rows that passed the context filter rather than scanning the full dataset, which can significantly reduce query time and produce the intended scoped top-N result.

**B** is incorrect because a context filter reduces the data scope but still generates a query to the data source; query cost is reduced, not eliminated.

**C** is incorrect because context filters work alongside other filters, not instead of them; other dimension and measure filters continue to apply on top of the context-filtered sub-table.

**D** is incorrect because a context filter is a query-planning instruction that affects how Tableau processes the current view; it does not change the connection type or create a persistent extract file.

questions.stem: Why should an analyst be cautious with packed bubbles or treemaps for exact comparisons?

options.text: Area-based encodings are harder to compare precisely than aligned lengths
They are the only option for quantitative data

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because human perception judges area differences less accurately than length differences along a common baseline; viewers can reliably compare bar heights side by side but struggle to judge whether one circle or rectangle is 20% or 30% larger than another, making packed bubbles and treemaps poor choices when precise ranking or exact value comparison is required.

**B** is incorrect because packed bubbles and treemaps do display dimensions; the dimension field defines which group each bubble or rectangle represents.

**C** is incorrect because packed bubbles and treemaps are one option among many for quantitative data; bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, and other types are equally valid depending on the analytical goal.

**D** is incorrect because tooltips work on all Tableau mark types including circles and rectangles; packing bubbles or using a treemap does not disable tooltip display.
questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. An analyst sees a field displayed as continuous on a shelf. How is it typically shown?

options.text: It is a published permission rule
It is always a table calculation
It is being used as a continuous field
It is hidden from the workbook

questions.explanation: A continuous field is correct because green pills typically indicate continuous values that create axes. Hidden fields, permissions, and table calculations are separate concepts.

questions.stem: When would a pivot in the Data Source page be most appropriate?

options.text: When wide month columns need to become attribute-value rows
When two database tables need an inner join
When a workbook must be published
When a continuous field should become discrete

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because pivoting in Tableau's Data Source page reshapes wide data—such as one column per month—into a long format with a single attribute column (Month) and a value column (Sales), which is the structure Tableau's shelf-based analysis expects.

**B** is incorrect because joining tables uses the join editor in the Data Source page canvas, not the pivot feature; inner join is a join type that keeps only matching rows.

**C** is incorrect because publishing is a server action performed through the File or Server menu after the workbook is built; it does not involve pivoting.

**D** is incorrect because changing a field from continuous to discrete is done by right-clicking the field pill in a view or in the Data pane; it is not performed through the Data Source pivot feature.
questions.reference: [Tableau Help: Pivot data from columns to rows](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/pivot.htm)

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which expression type calculates a value at a declared level such as customer or region regardless of the current view granularity?

options.text: A level of detail expression such as `FIXED`

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a FIXED LOD expression explicitly declares the dimension(s) at which to aggregate (e.g., `{FIXED [Customer] : SUM([Sales])}`) and computes that aggregate independently of whatever dimensions are on the shelves, allowing a customer-level total to appear in a view broken out by order date.

**B** is incorrect because a PNG export is a raster image file of the current view; it is not a calculation type and does not compute any values.

**C** is incorrect because a union stacks rows from multiple source tables; it is a data preparation operation unrelated to how aggregation granularity is controlled in calculations.

**D** is incorrect because a worksheet title is display text at the top of a view; it can reference certain field values but is not a calculation that computes aggregates at a declared grain.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. a certification-style scenario describes how field appears green in the view and creates a numeric axis. What does that most likely indicate?

options.text: It is a published permission rule
It is being used as a continuous field

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Tableau uses green to indicate continuous fields; a continuous field generates a quantitative axis that spans from the minimum to the maximum value rather than creating discrete headers for each value.

**B** is incorrect because permission rules are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud; they do not manifest as pill colors in a view.

**C** is incorrect because hidden worksheets are tabs that are not visible in presentation mode but still exist in the workbook; their hidden status is shown in the sheet tab row, not by pill color in the view.

**D** is incorrect because input tables are a Sigma Computing feature for writeback; they are not a Tableau concept, and no Tableau field type produces green pills to indicate input table status.

See [Tableau Help: Dimensions and measures](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/datafields_typesandroles_datatypes.htm).

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is the main purpose of Data Interpreter in Tableau?

options.text: It cleans common Excel formatting issues such as title rows and footers during import
It publishes extracts to Tableau Cloud automatically
It creates level of detail expressions

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Data Interpreter analyzes spreadsheets at import and removes common formatting artifacts—such as merged header cells, title rows, and footer text—that would otherwise appear as data rows, producing a cleaner field structure for analysis.

**A** is incorrect because subscription scheduling is a server-side feature in Tableau Cloud and Tableau Server that sends email snapshots to users; it is not a Data Interpreter capability.

**C** is incorrect because publishing extracts to Tableau Cloud is a separate step performed through the Publish menu after connecting to and preparing data; Data Interpreter is an import-time cleaning tool.

**D** is incorrect because level of detail (LOD) expressions are custom calculations written by the author; they are not generated by Data Interpreter.

See [Connect to Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/basicconnectoverview.htm).

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. why can `FIXED` LOD results differ from a simple aggregate in the current view?

options.text: Because LOD expressions are not aggregated
Because Tableau ignores the source data
Because the LOD can compute at a different declared granularity than the visible marks

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `FIXED [Customer] : SUM([Sales])` always computes the sum at the customer level regardless of whether the view is broken out by order date, region, or any other dimension; the view-level aggregation may be grouping the fixed values at a coarser grain, producing a different result from SUM([Sales]) which adapts to the current mark level of detail.

**B** is incorrect because FIXED LOD expressions query the same underlying source data as all other calculations; Tableau does not ignore source data when evaluating LODs.

**C** is incorrect because LOD expressions are aggregated—FIXED uses an explicit aggregate function (SUM, AVG, COUNT, etc.) within the expression; they are not unaggregated row-level values.

**D** is incorrect because the data type returned by a FIXED LOD is determined by the aggregate function used; SUM and AVG return numeric types, not text.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what is the main purpose of a context filter in Tableau?

options.text: It defines a primary filter that other filters can depend on

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a context filter creates a temporary sub-table of the data that is used as the starting point for all other filters and computations in the view; this is especially important for making top-N filters and set conditions work correctly within a specified subset.

**B** is incorrect because workbook publishing is performed through the Server or File menu; it is not related to context filters.

**C** is incorrect because changing a field between measure and dimension roles is done by right-clicking the field in the Data pane; a context filter applies to an already-classified field.

**D** is incorrect because unions are created in the Data Source page by stacking tables; a context filter is a view-level filter setting applied to an existing connected source.

questions.stem: A design review asks for justification. why should an analyst be cautious with packed bubbles or treemaps for exact comparisons?

options.text: Area-based encodings are harder to compare precisely than aligned lengths
They are the only option for quantitative data

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because human perception judges area differences less accurately than length differences along a common baseline; viewers can reliably compare bar heights side by side but struggle to judge whether one circle or rectangle is 20% or 30% larger than another, making packed bubbles and treemaps poor choices when precise ranking or exact value comparison is required.

**B** is incorrect because packed bubbles and treemaps do display dimensions; the dimension field defines which group each bubble or rectangle represents.

**C** is incorrect because packed bubbles and treemaps are one option among many for quantitative data; bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, and other types are equally valid depending on the analytical goal.

**D** is incorrect because tooltips work on all Tableau mark types including circles and rectangles; packing bubbles or using a treemap does not disable tooltip display.

See [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm).
questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which TWO chart choices commonly fit geographic analysis in Tableau? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because symbol maps plot a proportionally sized mark (typically a circle) at each geographic location, making it easy to compare magnitude across places while retaining spatial context.

**B** is correct because filled maps shade geographic polygons (countries, states, postal codes) by a measure's value, allowing viewers to see regional patterns through color intensity across defined boundaries.

**C** is incorrect because a union view is not a recognized Tableau chart type; a union is a data preparation operation that stacks rows, not a geographic visualization.

**D** is incorrect because a join chart is not a Tableau visualization type; a join is a data source operation that combines columns from related tables, not a type of map or chart.

questions.stem: A design review asks for justification. why would an analyst create a hierarchy of `Region > State > City`?

options.text: To enable drill-down and roll-up navigation
To create a data extract automatically

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a hierarchy defines an ordered path through related dimensions; users can click the + or - buttons on a hierarchy pill to expand from Region to State to City or collapse back up, enabling interactive geographic drill-through in any view.

**B** is incorrect because publishing to Tableau Reader is a sharing action determined by the file type (.twbx); creating a hierarchy does not trigger or affect publication.

**C** is incorrect because joining databases is a data source operation configured in the Data Source page; hierarchies are created in the Data pane from existing fields that are already connected.

**D** is incorrect because extract creation is a connection type choice; grouping fields into a hierarchy does not initiate or require an extract.

See [Tableau Help: Create a hierarchy](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/qs_hierarchies.htm).

questions.stem: A candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which TWO statements about measures in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Measures are often aggregated in views by default
Measures commonly represent quantitative values such as sales or profit
Measures can never be used in filters
Measures are always discrete blue pills

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because when a measure is placed on a shelf, Tableau automatically applies an aggregation (SUM by default for numeric fields) to summarize the values at the level of detail defined by the dimensions in the view.

**B** is correct because measures are numeric fields that represent quantities analysts want to aggregate, compare, or calculate—such as Sales, Profit, Quantity, or Order Count.

**C** is incorrect because measures can be filtered using measure filters, which allow you to filter by an aggregate value (e.g., show only rows where SUM(Sales) > 1000) or at the row level before aggregation.

**D** is incorrect because measures placed on shelves as continuous fields appear as green pills; discrete blue pills indicate fields used as discrete (typically dimensions). A measure can be converted to discrete, but it is not always blue by default.

See [Tableau Help: Dimensions and measures](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/datafields_typesandroles_datatypes.htm).

questions.stem: A team is designing a new solution. A workbook must query a changing operational source without creating an extract first. Which connection mode best fits?
questions.explanation: A live connection is correct because it queries the source system directly rather than using an extract copy. Story sheets, set actions, and packaged workbooks are unrelated to connection mode.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. a team is evaluating the next step. what is the main benefit of a context filter before a computationally expensive top-N filter?

options.text: It converts live data into an extract automatically
It can reduce the data considered by the later top-N logic

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a context filter materializes a temporary sub-table; when the top-N filter evaluates next, it operates only over the rows that passed the context filter rather than scanning the full dataset, which can significantly reduce query time and produce the intended scoped top-N result.

**B** is incorrect because a context filter reduces the data scope but still generates a query to the data source; query cost is reduced, not eliminated.

**C** is incorrect because context filters work alongside other filters, not instead of them; other dimension and measure filters continue to apply on top of the context-filtered sub-table.

**D** is incorrect because a context filter is a query-planning instruction that affects how Tableau processes the current view; it does not change the connection type or create a persistent extract file.

See [Tableau Help: Filters](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/filtering.htm).

questions.stem: During exam preparation, you evaluate this scenario. A user saves a `.twb` file instead of `.twbx`. What is the key limitation?

options.text: It does not bundle the local data files and related resources
It forces all fields to become dimensions

questions.explanation: A `.twb` limitation is correct because it stores workbook definitions but does not package local data files. It can still contain worksheets and calculated fields.

questions.stem: A candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which view is often best for showing correlation between two quantitative measures?

options.text: Stacked bar chart of one category only

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a scatter plot places one measure on the horizontal axis and a second on the vertical axis, with each mark representing an individual data point; the resulting point cloud reveals the direction, strength, and form of the relationship between the two measures.

**B** is incorrect because a stacked bar chart visualizes part-to-whole composition within a single categorical dimension; it does not plot two measures against each other in a way that reveals correlation.

**C** is incorrect because a text table without measures contains no quantitative values to compare; it cannot communicate correlation between numeric variables.

**D** is incorrect because a hierarchy control (the expand/collapse drill-down interface) is a navigation feature, not a chart type; it does not display data points in any layout that reveals correlation.

See [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm).
questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: Which TWO functions are commonly associated with table calculations rather than basic row-level string cleaning? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `RUNNING_SUM` is a table calculation function that accumulates a measure's aggregate value across the partitioned and ordered table partition, updating as each row is processed.

**B** is correct because `WINDOW_AVG` is a table calculation function that computes the average of a measure across a defined window of rows within the partition, operating on the already-aggregated values in the view rather than on raw source rows.

**C** is incorrect because `LEFT` is a string function that extracts a specified number of characters from the left of a string value; it operates at the row level on individual field values and is not a table calculation.

**D** is incorrect because `UPPER` is a string function that converts all characters in a text field to uppercase; like `LEFT`, it is a row-level transformation, not a table calculation that operates across the view partition.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. a candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which TWO statements about joins in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Using a join always stacks rows from one table under another
Joins can be used only with text files
Using a join combines columns from tables based on matching fields
Inner joins keep only records with matches on both sides

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because joins use a key relationship to horizontally combine fields from two or more tables, producing a wider result set where each row contains columns from all joined tables.

**B** is correct because an inner join filters the result to only include rows where the key value exists in both tables, discarding unmatched rows from either side.

**C** is incorrect because stacking rows is the behavior of a union, not a join; joins combine columns, not rows.

**D** is incorrect because Tableau supports joins across many source types including Excel, databases, and cloud warehouses; joins are not restricted to text files.

See [Tableau Help: Join your data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/joining_tables.htm).

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which Marks card property controls the hue used for marks?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Color property on the Marks card controls the hue, saturation, and opacity of marks; placing a dimension on Color assigns a distinct hue to each member, while placing a measure on Color applies a sequential or diverging color gradient.

**B** is incorrect because the Detail property adds a field to increase mark granularity without encoding it visually as size, color, or label; it adds more individual marks without changing their color.

**C** is incorrect because the Tooltip property makes a field available in the hover tooltip text; it does not affect the color or any other visual property of the marks.

**D** is incorrect because the Rows shelf defines what field creates row headers or the vertical axis structure of the view; it is not a Marks card property and does not control mark color.

questions.stem: What does sorting a dimension field change in a worksheet?

options.text: The order in which the members appear

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because sorting a dimension changes the sequence in which its members are rendered—alphabetical, by measure value ascending or descending, manually, or by nested sort—affecting only the display order, not the underlying data.

**B** is incorrect because publishing is a separate action performed through the Server or File menu; sorting a dimension does not trigger or affect publication.

**C** is incorrect because the data source is determined by the connection configured in the Data Source page; applying a sort to a dimension does not change where the data comes from.

**D** is incorrect because project permissions in Tableau Server or Cloud are managed by site administrators; they are not modified by how a dimension is sorted in a worksheet.

questions.stem: Why can `FIXED` LOD results differ from a simple aggregate in the current view?

options.text: Because the LOD can compute at a different declared granularity than the visible marks
Because Tableau ignores the source data
Because LOD expressions are not aggregated

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `FIXED [Customer] : SUM([Sales])` always computes the sum at the customer level regardless of whether the view is broken out by order date, region, or any other dimension; the view-level aggregation may be grouping the fixed values at a coarser grain, producing a different result from SUM([Sales]) which adapts to the current mark level of detail.

**B** is incorrect because FIXED LOD expressions query the same underlying source data as all other calculations; Tableau does not ignore source data when evaluating LODs.

**C** is incorrect because LOD expressions are aggregated—FIXED uses an explicit aggregate function (SUM, AVG, COUNT, etc.) within the expression; they are not unaggregated row-level values.

**D** is incorrect because the data type returned by a FIXED LOD is determined by the aggregate function used; SUM and AVG return numeric types, not text.

questions.stem: A candidate reviews a workbook requirement. What best describes the role distinction between dimensions and measures?

options.text: Dimensions are always numeric and measures are always text
Dimensions cannot appear on the Marks card
Dimensions usually segment or label data, while measures are typically aggregated quantitative values

questions.explanation: Dimensions-versus-measures is correct because dimensions often partition data and measures are quantitative fields that can be aggregated. The other statements are oversimplified or false.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about joins in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Inner joins keep only records with matches on both sides
A join always stacks rows from one table under another
Joins can be used only with text files
A join combines columns from tables based on matching fields

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because joins use a key relationship to horizontally combine fields from two or more tables, producing a wider result set where each row contains columns from all joined tables.

**B** is correct because an inner join filters the result to only include rows where the key value exists in both tables, discarding unmatched rows from either side.

**C** is incorrect because stacking rows is the behavior of a union, not a join; joins combine columns, not rows.

**D** is incorrect because Tableau supports joins across many source types including Excel, databases, and cloud warehouses; joins are not restricted to text files.

See [Tableau Help](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/joining_tables.htm) for the documented behavior.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to avoid a common mistake. Which platform is commonly used to publish Tableau workbooks for browser-based consumption?
questions.explanation: Tableau Server or Tableau Cloud is correct because published workbooks can be consumed interactively in a browser there. Screenshots, hidden tabs, and the Data Source page do not publish interactive content.

questions.stem: An analyst needs the most appropriate next step. A source contains related tables that should remain separate logical tables instead of being flattened prematurely. Which modeling concept is most relevant in modern Tableau?
questions.explanation: Relationships are correct because they connect logical tables while preserving separate table context until query time. Cross joins, story points, and duplication do not provide that semantic modeling approach.

questions.stem: A candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which shelf on the Marks card controls whether text labels appear on marks?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because placing a field on the Label shelf of the Marks card adds that field's value as text displayed directly on or near each mark; the Label shelf also provides options to control label alignment, font, and visibility conditions.

**B** is incorrect because the Pages shelf creates a page-by-page animation or flip book of the view broken out by the values of the field placed there; it controls animated playback, not text labeling.

**C** is incorrect because the Filters shelf restricts which rows of data are included in the view; it does not control text annotation on marks.

**D** is incorrect because the Columns shelf determines what fields define the horizontal axis or column headers; it is a view structure shelf, not a mark-level labeling control.

See [Control the Appearance of Marks in the View](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/viewparts_marks_markproperties.htm).
questions.reference: [Control the Appearance of Marks in the View](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/viewparts_marks_markproperties.htm)

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which export option is best when a user wants a static image of a dashboard for a slide deck?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because exporting as PNG (via the Worksheet or Dashboard > Export > Image menu) produces a raster image file of the current view that can be inserted directly into a PowerPoint or Keynote slide without any additional Tableau software required by viewers.

**B** is incorrect because a union is a data preparation operation that stacks rows from multiple tables; it produces data, not an exportable image.

**C** is incorrect because a context filter is a query-scoping setting that affects which data the view uses; it is not an export format.

**D** is incorrect because a level of detail expression is a calculation type that controls aggregation granularity; it is not an output format for presentations.

See [Tableau Help](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/export.htm) for the documented behavior.
